draft: Put on the attacker's hat from Modules 11–12. Read the vulnerable search query, substitute the payload by hand, then decide what actually fixes it and what merely caps the damage.

fields.label: Inkwell's title search is built as "SELECT title, price FROM book WHERE title LIKE '%" + search + "%'". Why is it injectable?
options: The user's input is concatenated into the query string, so an attacker can close the quote and inject their own SQL — the input becomes code
LIKE is an inherently unsafe operator that should never be used
SELECT should never be run against a table that also holds prices
The % wildcards let the attacker read arbitrary files from disk

fields.label: What is the correct fix for that search query?
options: Parameterize it: WHERE title LIKE ? and pass '%' + search + '%' as a bound value, so the input can never be parsed as code
Strip the words UNION and SELECT out of the search text before running it
Escape every quote in the search text and keep concatenating
Reject any search longer than 20 characters

fields.label: Even after parameterizing, why grant the website's database account only SELECT on the catalog and SELECT, INSERT on orders — and nothing on staff_user?
options: Least privilege — if an injection or stolen credential ever hits that account, the attacker inherits only those few rights and can't read staff logins, delete orders, or drop tables
It makes the storefront's queries run measurably faster
It prevents SQL injection from ever happening on that account
It is required or the CREATE VIEW in Stage 6 will not run

fields.label: Match each part of the security pass to the role it plays.
fields.help: Only one of these removes the vulnerability; the others limit the damage or hide the data.
options: Caps the blast radius if an account is compromised
Exposes only safe columns and hides customer/staff tables
